content.body: what is our policy on test262 tests generated with LLMs? looks like the first test262 PR openly using one has just come in
it's not huge and at first glance seems useful, so I'm not concerned about slop in this case
I'm willing to review it, in other words
but I don't want to do so if there is some copyright concern within ECMA that overrides me 😄
If it's the zip one it is not actually good
The existing tests cover what it claims not to be testing
I think a policy of blindly rejecting LLM PRs from randoms will save time for everyone
on the topic of LLMs writing test262 tests, I'm kinda tempted to ask one to port the tests from the iterator includes repo to test262 tests
it should be about as straightforward as it gets, and if it succeeds, it will cover like 80% of the tests
but I also don't know exactly where we draw the line on this stuff
like if it was coming up with novel tests, I wouldn't want to subject that on reviewers
but if it's just porting the tests I wrote by hand and I review the PR first before submitting, it seems harmless
* it should be about as straightforward as it gets, and if it succeeds, it will cover like 80% of the necessary tests

m.new_content.body: it should be about as straightforward as it gets, and if it succeeds, it will cover like 80% of the necessary tests

content.body: * it should be about as straightforward as it gets, and if it succeeds, it will cover like >80% of the necessary tests

content.body: I bet you could get it to write a codemod and then reuse it on your future proposals

content.body: ah, ok, I meant https://github.com/tc39/test262/pull/4984

https://github.com/tc39/test262/pull/4996 is more reasonable because it's at least by someone with some tangential reason to care, not just someone doing a spray-and-pray over every open source project in the world
strictly speaking our AI usage policy does say not to do this, though
